history: The client has experienced chronic depression and isolation since late adolescence, with longstanding difficulties forming and maintaining friendships. A recent breakup triggered a relapse into a depressive episode, leading to medication adjustments. The client often immerses themselves in intellectual pursuits, such as mathematics and teaching, which provide temporary relief and a sense of accomplishment but also highlight their ongoing social isolation. There is a pattern of avoidance and withdrawal, particularly when faced with emotional distress or tasks that feel overwhelming.
intermediate_belief: If I can't focus or complete my planned tasks, it means I'm failing and there's something wrong with me. If I don't accomplish what I set out to do, others will see me as incapable or lazy. I must always be productive and intellectually engaged to prove my worth and avoid feeling the pain of isolation.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I can't even do the things I planned, it shows that I'm hopeless and nothing will ever get better. When I fail to engage, it confirms that I'm stuck and there's no point in trying.
coping_strategies: The client withdraws and avoids tasks or social interactions when distressed, often turning to passive activities like watching television. They immerse themselves in intellectual pursuits or teaching as a way to escape emotional discomfort and gain a sense of validation, but this also reinforces their isolation. Physical activity is occasionally used for temporary relief, but avoidance and rumination remain dominant.
situation: Planned a weekend for sewing and catching up on work tasks. Was unable to focus on familiar tasks and spent the day watching television.
auto_thought: I can't even do the simple things I planned. This just proves I'm never going to get better.
behavior: I avoided sewing and work tasks. I spent the day watching television instead of doing what I planned. I withdrew from any effort to engage with my goals.
